paper_title: Incremental Learning of Temporally-Coherent Gaussian Mixture Models
core_contribution: An incremental learning algorithm for Gaussian Mixture Models that processes data points one-by-one rather than in blocks, using only two GMMs in memory (current and historical) with no stored historical data. The method introduces the concept of a Historical GMM to enable dynamic model complexity selection through component splitting and merging based on expected description length.
algorithm_steps: Step 1: Fixed-complexity update - update current GMM parameters using only the new data point and existing model parameters, assuming component likelihoods don't change much
Step 2: Model splitting - create new Gaussian components by computing 'difference' components between current GMM and Historical GMM of same complexity
Step 3: Component merging - use MDL criterion with expected description length to decide whether to merge or split components, comparing costs of merge vs split operations
key_equations: Eq 7-8: Fixed complexity parameter updates using only current parameters and component likelihoods
Eq 9-10: Difference component parameters computed from current and historical GMMs
Eq 11-12: MDL criterion for model selection using expected description length
Eq 13-14: Expected likelihood computation using Bhattacharyya distance for Gaussian components

key_assumptions.assumption: Temporal coherence - successive data points vary smoothly with time according to a unimodal density
key_assumptions.why_needed: Enables the assumption that component likelihoods don't change much when adding new points, allowing parameter updates without storing historical data
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When data arrives in random order or with large temporal jumps, breaking the smooth variation assumption

key_assumptions.assumption: Component likelihoods remain approximately constant when adding new data points
key_assumptions.why_needed: Allows incremental parameter updates using only current model parameters without recomputing likelihoods for all historical data
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When new data points are very far from existing components or when the underlying distribution changes significantly

baseline_limitations_identified: Previous methods assume data arrives in blocks rather than one-by-one, require storing historical data or multiple GMMs, and don't exploit temporal coherence in data arrival order
proposed_improvement: Processes single data points incrementally using only two GMMs in memory, exploits temporal coherence for efficient updates, and uses Historical GMM concept for dynamic complexity selection
condition_where_baseline_wins: When data arrives in large batches with sufficient memory available, or when temporal ordering is completely random without any coherence structure
datasets_used: Video sequences of faces in realistic imaging conditions
suitable_toy_datasets: 2D Gaussian mixture synthetic data with temporal ordering
Synthetic time series with smooth transitions
toy_dataset_justification: These datasets would exhibit the temporal coherence property required by the method while being simple enough to visualize and validate the incremental learning behavior
reproducibility_notes: Algorithm is clearly described but implementation details for Historical GMM management and exact MDL computation thresholds may require careful tuning

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Provides reference point for detecting when new components should be created through difference computation
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Would lose ability to dynamically adjust model complexity, reducing to fixed-complexity incremental EM

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Allows assumption that component likelihoods don't change much, enabling parameter updates without historical data
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Would require storing all historical data to properly compute likelihood changes, defeating the memory efficiency goal

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Decides whether to merge or split components for dynamic complexity selection
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Would need alternative criterion for complexity selection, potentially leading to over/under-fitting

known_failure_modes.scenario: Data arrives in completely random order without temporal structure
known_failure_modes.reason: Violates temporal coherence assumption, making component likelihood stability assumption invalid
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Temporal coherence - successive data points vary smoothly with time

known_failure_modes.scenario: Underlying distribution changes rapidly or has sudden mode switches
known_failure_modes.reason: Historical GMM becomes outdated quickly, leading to poor component splitting decisions
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Component likelihoods remain approximately constant when adding new data points
